policy_statement: Establish fee structures for residential waste and recycling services that encourage waste reduction.
mechanism_description: Adjust residential waste and recycling service fees to encourage waste reduction, decreasing landfill disposal and avoiding landfill methane emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Pricing signal → ↓ landfilled waste (↓ degradable organics in landfill) → ↓ anaerobic decomposition → ↓ CH₄ emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in human activity/system: Municipal pricing for residential waste service shifts from flat fees to volume/weight-based or tiered fees, increasing the marginal cost of trash disposal and incentivizing waste prevention, recycling, and composting.  
2) Mediating physical process: With less biodegradable material entering landfills, anaerobic decomposition in landfill cells is reduced, lowering methane generation (and associated fugitive emissions not captured by gas systems).  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Net reduction in GHG emissions, dominated by avoided CH₄ from landfills (with potential secondary effects from increased recycling/composting and reduced upstream production emissions).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced landfill methane emissions due to lower quantities of waste—especially organics—being disposed in landfills.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: PAYT pricing primarily reduces landfill disposal, which reduces methane emissions from anaerobic decomposition—this is a direct GHG mitigation pathway.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While the mechanism can also improve material efficiency (less waste generation) and increase recycling, the largest climate signal for this mechanism as stated is landfill CH₄ avoidance (explicit in the canonical mechanism).  
3) Edge cases applied: If PAYT causes illegal dumping or burning, benefits can be reduced or reversed; if a jurisdiction already captures nearly all landfill gas and has minimal organics in trash, the incremental methane benefit is smaller. These do not change the intended dominant mechanism category.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any policy that uses PAYT-style pricing to reduce landfill disposal is fundamentally aimed at avoiding landfill methane via reduced waste landfilling, which is mitigation.  
5) Note on subtypes: Any secondary subtype assignment (e.g., “waste prevention” vs “recycling diversion” emphasis, or inclusion of organics collection) must be determined from policy-level evidence and is deferred.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Potential rebound/side-effects include illegal dumping, contamination of recycling streams, or shifting waste to jurisdictions without controls; benefits are also lower where landfill gas capture is highly effective or the marginal reduction is mostly inert waste rather than organics.
key_indicators: "Establish fee structures"; "residential waste and recycling services"; "encourage the reduction of waste"
co_benefits: Reduced local air pollutants/odors from landfill operations; extended landfill life and reduced municipal solid-waste management costs; potential litter reduction from improved diversion (depending on anti-dumping measures)
instance_edge_case_notes: Does not specify the exact pricing design (e.g., pay-as-you-throw per bag/weight vs tiered cart subscriptions), but the presence of “fee structures” intended to “encourage” reduction is sufficient to classify as a pricing mechanism.
